author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: you need a binder that is compatible with the wax. The gum arabic is water based. Try an oil based microporous varnish

author: @Venturestarx
text: No need for apologies, it was rather funny. And I got some neat ideas to mess around.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: there is an elemental analysis being done as we speak and I will post the results as soon as I get them. As for toxicity - they have been digging this stuff up for hundreds of years - one company even coats cheese in it - I don't think you really need to worry about that - But if you are just wait for the analysis or wear gloves

author: @MrD4N1EL7
text:
This special kind of graphite would be nice to experiment with, but what is the chemical composition of this mineral considering it doesn't just contain graphite. Are the other minerals toxic? There is no reference to any other mineral compositions on the website other than graphite which is quite a concern.
Thanks in advance.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: sorry mate - I was being just a little sarcastic - these things happen - I can't tell you how many things I have blown up. The only thing to do is start again - you have my sympathy

author: @Venturestarx
text: Also shoot me an email with what you did, I'll get on it over the weekend.

author: @Venturestarx
text: Yea Dan I'm still trying to use the film. The microwave killed it because of arcing, and my laser rig isn't up to spec. I'm burning holes in everything even though a good reduction forms.

author: @Venturestarx
text: There is no result for a Liter on the floor, except a very sad face and loss of brownie points. I cleaned it up and got a small shard of glass in my hand, but I did recover some of it to make a neat film decal. I posted a video.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: dude! - result!

author: @Venturestarx
text: Guess who just dropped a Liter of Graphene solution on the floor. :(

author: @Venturestarx
text: I'll check that out. Like I said, my best results were on glass. I had some good results on Aluminum foil, but the carbon wipes off with the slightest touch. I asked a professor, his reply is the oxide layer on the foil won't let it stick.

author: @DanielbenDaniel
text: Venturestarx Did you use that film I sent you? My GO tested out almost identical to yours. I finally had a good batch.  I light scribe that film on CD with very high resistant? The only way I'v had good reduction thus far is that Bio Char cloth method I posted on Roberts Super caps video.  I am moving on to Inks now. Should have some results next week or 2.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I'll give it a go heating up some here and see what happens

author: @Venturestarx
text: 190 C (ish) I'm in the states so 395 F

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: it contains graphite which is a susceptor for microwave radiation so I doubt it would withstand microwave irradiation but what is the oven temp you use?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: it contains graphite which is a susceptor for microwave radiation so I doubt it would withstand microwave irradiation but what is the oven temp you use?

author: @Venturestarx
text: I use microwave reduction for the graphene. The oven works pretty good as well, but only glass seems to withstand the process. If I had a graphite substrate like this, I may get stellar results.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I don't know - I haven't tried - why do you want to microwave your batteries if I might ask?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text:
Lol,

I am pretty sure it is the inclusions that makes the graphite here so odd and so conductive - like you I can't wait we have sent several samples off but as you know they don't rush themselves at the labs - but as soon as I get info mate - i'll be passing it on.

author: @Venturestarx
text: Is that ink durable to some degree? I'm thinking about it as the plate in my battery, because all of the other substrates aren't doing well in the microwave or the oven.

author: @DanielbenDaniel
text:
Pretty kewl Robert. I am still waiting for u to send my ticket to Sri Lankan?
I made some good ink, best result thus far is 5 oms per 1/4" just cant come up with a usable product as of yet... That graphite chunk you are holding looks like it has been bonded with some type of metal mineral, I bought a souvenir rock that looks just like it at a Missouri cave years ago.  Cant wait to here the compounds in it. Have fun!

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Lol - I am making the stuff by the gallon mate - gonna send some off soon for analysis

author: @jdubeau007
text:
wow, not only are you a mind reader, you must be a time traveler. 
Northwestern University Inkjet-printed graphene
Now Northwestern has devised a new method, using ethanol and ethyl cellulose,
that can be used to mass produce flakes of fairly high quality graphene.

author: @zooglesqueeze
text: I've done a lot of screen printing and it seems interesting, but don't know a lot about conductivity. I guess it's a question of experimentation. If you used a binder that was solvent based it might evaporate and leave the graphite behind. You could experiment with PVA in the short term.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: will do mate cheers

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Well, you could - but it would reduce the conductivity - the better thing would be to specifically formulate a screen printing ink for you to maximise the conductivity - is this something you are interested in?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: You're right I am - cheers for that mate.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Lol - it's ok mate. All graphite is diamagnetic to a degree so the answer is yes but not as well as the actual diamagnetic graphite as the ink isn't made from that

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: cheers mate - you are a star

author: @Slider2732
text:
While it's in a bottle and therefore won't dry out or evaporate, the storage method is ideal. You'll know the chemical chain to attack :) Am thinking a small amount of rubber cement is the obvious choice and wonder if it can be diluted with an alcohol perhaps for solution suspension, then the alcohol would rapidly evaporate when applied to the bendy surface.
If it works, gimme a shout out on a resulting vid  lol

author: @mwm2929
text: Sooo...you are going to have numerous patentable proccesses from this material.  You should by all means keep one for yourself.  Liscense out the patent to a large electronics firm for 20 years.  The US patent law was changed recently to stop this kind of tech getting out.  Now it isn't who built it first, it is who filed for patent first!  This stops people from making most things given away on youtube into commercial production.  The oil and gas companies have a team of patent filers watching.

author: @mwm2929
text: You are having a great time I take it!  All the projects and tests, so little time!
